text: Chess masters perceive board positions differently than novices — not as collections of individual pieces but as meaningful configurations. Adriaan de Groot's 1940s studies found that grandmasters could reconstruct a mid-game position from a five-second glance, while beginners recalled only a handful of pieces. Yet when pieces were placed randomly, the grandmasters' advantage vanished. This proved they were not seeing better; they were recognizing patterns built from thousands of hours of deliberate study. Herbert Simon later estimated grandmasters hold roughly fifty thousand such patterns in long-term memory. Expertise, then, is largely a library of perceptual chunks — rapid recognition structures assembled only through sustained, focused engagement with a domain.
question: What did de Groot's experiments reveal about the chess grandmasters' memory advantage for board positions?

options.text: It disappeared when pieces were arranged randomly, proving the advantage was pattern recognition, not superior memory
It persisted even with random positions, confirming they had genuinely superior working memory
It was most pronounced in endgame positions rather than mid-game configurations
It was limited to pieces they had personally played in competitive matches
